# Hook Templates: Concrete Examples by Formula and Audience

Detailed hook examples for social media content generation. Each example includes the hook text, formula type, target audience, recommended framework pairing, and a brief explanation of why it works.

---

## Stat-Led Formula

### Example 1

**Hook:**
> 87% of founders spend their first hour on email.
>
> Only 4% say it's the best use of their time.

**Formula:** Stat-Led
**Audience:** Founder-CEO
**Framework pairing:** How-To, Listicle
**Why it works:** The juxtaposition between 87% doing it and 4% valuing it creates instant cognitive dissonance. The reader needs to know the alternative.

### Example 2

**Hook:**
> We reduced deployment failures by 91%.
>
> The fix wasn't better testing. It was deleting 40% of our test suite.

**Formula:** Stat-Led
**Audience:** Technical
**Framework pairing:** Contrarian Take, Personal Lesson
**Why it works:** The stat is impressive, but the counterintuitive cause (fewer tests = fewer failures) is the real scroll-stopper. Technical audiences respect specificity and will read to verify the claim.

### Example 3

**Hook:**
> Our best-performing ad last quarter had zero design assets.
>
> Plain text. White background. 3.2x ROAS.

**Formula:** Stat-Led
**Audience:** Marketer
**Framework pairing:** Contrarian Take, Personal Lesson
**Why it works:** Marketers invest heavily in creative production. The idea that plain text outperformed designed assets challenges their daily work and demands explanation.

### Cross-Platform Examples

### Example 3a (X/Twitter)

**Hook:**
> 73% of SaaS churn happens in the first 14 days. Your onboarding is the product.

**Formula:** Stat-Led
**Audience:** Founder-CEO
**Platform:** X/Twitter
**Why it works:** Fits in a single tweet with room to spare. The stat delivers the insight and the second sentence reframes it into an actionable mental model -- no thread needed to land the point.

### Example 3b (Meta -- Facebook)

**Hook:**
> We tracked every customer touchpoint for 6 months. The result that surprised us most: 68% of our revenue came from people who never opened a single marketing email.

**Formula:** Stat-Led
**Audience:** Marketer
**Platform:** Meta (Facebook)
**Why it works:** Storytelling wrapper around a stat plays to Facebook's engagement patterns. The emotional surprise element ("surprised us most") drives comments and shares from marketers re-evaluating their own email dependence.

### Example 3c (TikTok)

**Hook:**
> You're wasting 40% of your ad budget. Here's the number that proves it.

**Formula:** Stat-Led
**Audience:** Founder-CEO
**Platform:** TikTok
**Why it works:** Direct address ("You're") with a specific percentage in the first three words. The promise of proof creates a reason to keep watching. Short, punchy, and impossible to scroll past without feeling the sting.

---

## Question Formula

### Example 4

**Hook:**
> What would you build if you had 10 extra hours every week?
>
> Not 10 extra hours of meetings. 10 hours of deep, uninterrupted building time.

**Formula:** Question
**Audience:** Founder-CEO
**Framework pairing:** How-To, Personal Lesson
**Why it works:** The clarifying second line transforms a generic aspiration into something viscerally desirable. Founders drowning in meetings feel this immediately.

### Example 5

**Hook:**
> What if 60% of your Kubernetes pods are running for no reason?
>
> We audited ours. They were.

**Formula:** Question
**Audience:** Technical
**Framework pairing:** How-To, Industry Insight
**Why it works:** Asks a question that triggers immediate anxiety, then confirms the worst case is real. Engineers will read to see the audit methodology and compare it to their own infrastructure.

### Example 6

**Hook:**
> What's the one metric your board actually cares about?
>
> I asked 23 CEOs. The answer wasn't revenue.

**Formula:** Question
**Audience:** Corporate-CXO
**Framework pairing:** Industry Insight, Listicle
**Why it works:** CXOs live in board dynamics. The specificity of "23 CEOs" signals primary research, and withholding the answer creates an irresistible gap.

### Cross-Platform Examples

### Example 6a (X/Twitter)

**Hook:**
> Why do the best engineers you know mass-delete Slack channels every quarter?

**Formula:** Question
**Audience:** Technical
**Platform:** X/Twitter
**Why it works:** Under 80 characters. The question implies a pattern the reader hasn't noticed, creating an immediate curiosity gap. "The best engineers you know" triggers identification -- the reader wants to be in that group.

### Example 6b (Meta -- Instagram)

**Hook:**
> What would your business look like if you only worked on it 3 days a week?

**Formula:** Question
**Audience:** Founder-CEO
**Platform:** Meta (Instagram)
**Why it works:** Under 125 characters. Taps into the aspirational lifestyle content that performs on Instagram. The specificity of "3 days" makes it feel achievable, not hypothetical. Drives comments as founders share their own answers.

### Example 6c (TikTok)

**Hook:**
> Wait -- you're still doing your own bookkeeping?

**Formula:** Question
**Audience:** Founder-CEO
**Platform:** TikTok
**Why it works:** "Wait" as the first word is a pattern interrupt layered on a question. Direct address and the implied judgment ("still") creates mild social anxiety that keeps viewers watching for the alternative.

---

## Story Formula

### Example 7

**Hook:**
> Last Thursday, I almost sent a $180,000 proposal to the wrong client.
>
> The mistake I caught taught me more about systems than any book on operations.

**Formula:** Story
**Audience:** Founder-CEO
**Framework pairing:** Personal Lesson, Story
**Why it works:** The near-miss with a specific dollar amount creates stakes. "Almost" keeps it relatable rather than catastrophic, and the pivot to "systems" promises actionable takeaway, not just confession.

### Example 8

**Hook:**
> My marketing manager quit on a Tuesday.
>
> By Friday, our pipeline was up 15%. Here's what that taught me about single points of failure.

**Formula:** Story
**Audience:** Founder-CEO
**Framework pairing:** Personal Lesson, Contrarian Take
**Why it works:** The expected outcome is pipeline collapse. The reversal (pipeline went up) is genuinely surprising and raises uncomfortable questions about role necessity that founders navigate constantly.

### Example 9

**Hook:**
> I spent three weeks building a custom analytics dashboard.
>
> Then an intern found the same data in a Google Sheet we already had.

**Formula:** Story
**Audience:** Technical
**Framework pairing:** Personal Lesson, Story
**Why it works:** Every engineer has over-built something. The specificity of "three weeks" and the deflation of "an intern found it" makes this painfully relatable. The humor softens the vulnerability.

### Cross-Platform Examples

### Example 9a (X/Twitter)

**Hook:**
> Fired our most expensive contractor last month. Revenue went up. The lesson was brutal.

**Formula:** Story
**Audience:** Founder-CEO
**Platform:** X/Twitter
**Why it works:** Three short sentences, each escalating. Under 90 characters. The reversal (fired + revenue up) demands explanation, and "brutal" signals the lesson won't be sugar-coated. Drives quote tweets and replies.

### Example 9b (Meta -- Facebook)

**Hook:**
> Three years ago I was sitting in my car in the office parking lot, too anxious to walk inside. I'd just realized our biggest client was about to leave and it was entirely my fault.

**Formula:** Story
**Audience:** Founder-CEO
**Platform:** Meta (Facebook)
**Why it works:** Emotional vulnerability and scene-setting dominate on Facebook. The physical detail ("sitting in my car") creates a vivid image. The self-blame ("entirely my fault") is raw enough to stop the scroll and generate empathetic engagement.

### Example 9c (TikTok)

**Hook:**
> Story time: I got a call from my accountant that made me close my laptop and just stare at the wall.

**Formula:** Story
**Audience:** Founder-CEO
**Platform:** TikTok
**Why it works:** "Story time" is a recognized TikTok format trigger. The visceral detail ("close my laptop and stare at the wall") creates immediate emotional investment. The withholding of what the accountant said is a textbook open loop for video content.

---

## Contrarian Formula

### Example 10

**Hook:**
> Stop A/B testing your landing pages.
>
> At your traffic level, the results are statistically meaningless.

**Formula:** Contrarian
**Audience:** Marketer
**Framework pairing:** Contrarian Take, Industry Insight
**Why it works:** A/B testing is gospel in marketing. Challenging it with a statistical argument (sample size) hits marketers where they are most data-literate, making the contrarian claim hard to dismiss without reading the full argument.

### Example 11

**Hook:**
> Stop hiring senior engineers.
>
> Your codebase doesn't need seniority. It needs consistency.

**Formula:** Contrarian
**Audience:** Founder-CEO
**Framework pairing:** Contrarian Take, Personal Lesson
**Why it works:** Hiring senior engineers is default founder behavior. The reframe from "seniority" to "consistency" is a genuinely useful mental model shift that founders can evaluate against their own team.

### Example 12

**Hook:**
> Forget about thought leadership.
>
> Your clients don't need your thoughts. They need your systems.

**Formula:** Contrarian
**Audience:** Corporate-CXO
**Framework pairing:** Contrarian Take, Industry Insight
**Why it works:** "Thought leadership" is deeply embedded in CXO vocabulary. Dismissing it with the direct "they need your systems" reframe is specific enough to feel actionable, not just provocative.

### Cross-Platform Examples

### Example 12a (X/Twitter)

**Hook:**
> Hot take: your product roadmap is a fiction document and everyone on your team knows it.

**Formula:** Contrarian
**Audience:** Founder-CEO
**Platform:** X/Twitter
**Why it works:** "Hot take" works on X because the platform rewards sharp opinions. "Fiction document" is a memorable reframe. The kicker ("everyone on your team knows it") adds social proof pressure. Under 100 characters and designed for quote-tweet debate.

### Example 12b (Meta -- Facebook)

**Hook:**
> I stopped setting goals last year. Not because I gave up -- because I finally realized goals were the thing holding me back from actually getting things done.

**Formula:** Contrarian
**Audience:** Founder-CEO
**Platform:** Meta (Facebook)
**Why it works:** The personal confession format suits Facebook's engagement style. The reversal ("goals were holding me back") challenges a universal assumption. The emotional framing ("finally realized") invites comments from people who agree or disagree.

### Example 12c (TikTok)

**Hook:**
> You need to stop networking. Seriously. It's the biggest waste of time for early-stage founders.

**Formula:** Contrarian
**Audience:** Founder-CEO
**Platform:** TikTok
**Why it works:** Direct address ("You need to") in the first three words is TikTok's strongest hook pattern. "Seriously" adds conviction. Challenges something every founder is told to do, guaranteeing engagement from people who want to argue or agree.

---

## Bold Claim Formula

### Example 13

**Hook:**
> The best founders I know all do the same thing before 7 AM.
>
> It's not meditation. It's not email. It's the most boring habit you'll ever adopt.

**Formula:** Bold Claim
**Audience:** Founder-CEO
**Framework pairing:** Listicle, Personal Lesson, How-To
**Why it works:** "The best founders I know" establishes social proof. Eliminating the expected answers (meditation, email) narrows the gap and increases curiosity. "Most boring habit" is a pattern interrupt within the bold claim -- boringness is not usually sold as a feature.

### Example 14

**Hook:**
> The most underpriced service in B2B right now is fractional CFO work.
>
> At $2-4k/month, it's the highest-ROI hire a founder under $5M ARR can make.

**Formula:** Bold Claim
**Audience:** Founder-CEO
**Framework pairing:** Industry Insight, How-To
**Why it works:** Specificity on price range and revenue threshold signals real market knowledge. Founders under $5M ARR -- a large LinkedIn audience -- immediately evaluate whether this applies to them.

### Example 15

**Hook:**
> Every company I've consulted for in the last two years has the same Slack problem.
>
> It's not notification overload. It's channel sprawl, and it's costing more than anyone measures.

**Formula:** Bold Claim
**Audience:** Corporate-CXO
**Framework pairing:** Industry Insight, Contrarian Take
**Why it works:** "Every company" is a strong universal claim that CXOs will read to either validate or challenge. Naming "channel sprawl" as the specific (and less obvious) problem differentiates this from generic Slack complaints.

### Cross-Platform Examples

### Example 15a (X/Twitter)

**Hook:**
> The single best hiring decision a startup can make has nothing to do with who you hire.

**Formula:** Bold Claim
**Audience:** Founder-CEO
**Platform:** X/Twitter
**Why it works:** The paradox ("best hiring decision" + "nothing to do with who you hire") creates an irresistible curiosity gap in a single sentence. Under 90 characters. The implicit mystery drives replies and quote tweets asking for the answer.

### Example 15b (Meta -- Instagram)

**Hook:**
> The most productive people I know never use to-do lists. Here's what they do instead.

**Formula:** Bold Claim
**Audience:** Founder-CEO
**Platform:** Meta (Instagram)
**Why it works:** Under 125 characters. The claim challenges a productivity staple, and "Here's what they do instead" promises a concrete alternative in the caption or carousel. Works especially well paired with a visual list or infographic.

### Example 15c (TikTok)

**Hook:**
> Your morning routine is sabotaging your business and I can prove it in 60 seconds.

**Formula:** Bold Claim
**Audience:** Founder-CEO
**Platform:** TikTok
**Why it works:** Direct address, bold claim, and a time-bound promise ("60 seconds") all in the first sentence. The confidence of "I can prove it" raises the stakes -- viewers stay to see if the creator delivers. Perfect for short-form video pacing.

---

## Pattern Interrupt Formula

### Example 16

**Hook:**
> Delete your to-do list.
>
> Not tomorrow. Right now. I'll explain.

**Formula:** Pattern Interrupt
**Audience:** Founder-CEO
**Framework pairing:** Contrarian Take, Personal Lesson
**Why it works:** The command is short, unexpected, and actionable. "Right now" increases urgency. "I'll explain" is the minimal curiosity bridge -- just enough to justify tapping "see more."

### Example 17

**Hook:**
> Fire your best-performing sales rep.
>
> (Don't actually do it. But read this before their next 1:1.)

**Formula:** Pattern Interrupt
**Audience:** Founder-CEO
**Framework pairing:** Contrarian Take, Story
**Why it works:** The retraction in the second line transforms shock into curiosity. The reader knows it is a setup but still wants to understand the insight. The parenthetical creates an intimate, conversational tone.

### Example 18

**Hook:**
> Your CI pipeline is lying to you.
>
> Green checks. Zero confidence. Here's how to tell the difference.

**Formula:** Pattern Interrupt
**Audience:** Technical
**Framework pairing:** How-To, Industry Insight
**Why it works:** "Lying to you" personifies a tool engineers trust implicitly. "Green checks. Zero confidence." is a three-word diagnosis that every engineer who has been burned by flaky tests will recognize instantly.

### Example 19

**Hook:**
> Unsubscribe from every marketing newsletter.
>
> Then rebuild your list with these 5 criteria. The signal-to-noise ratio changes everything.

**Formula:** Pattern Interrupt
**Audience:** Marketer
**Framework pairing:** Listicle, Contrarian Take
**Why it works:** Telling marketers to unsubscribe from marketing content is self-aware and unexpected. The immediate follow-up with "5 criteria" promises a rebuild framework, turning destruction into construction.

### Cross-Platform Examples

### Example 19a (X/Twitter)

**Hook:**
> Close your analytics dashboard. You're optimizing for the wrong metric.

**Formula:** Pattern Interrupt
**Audience:** Marketer
**Platform:** X/Twitter
**Why it works:** Two punchy sentences under 70 characters total. The command ("Close your analytics") is jarring for data-driven marketers, and the follow-up ("wrong metric") implies the author knows which metric is right. Drives argumentative quote tweets.

### Example 19b (Meta -- Facebook)

**Hook:**
> I deleted every automation in my business last month. What happened next completely changed how I think about scaling.

**Formula:** Pattern Interrupt
**Audience:** Founder-CEO
**Platform:** Meta (Facebook)
**Why it works:** The drastic action ("deleted every automation") creates shock value, and the personal narrative framing plays to Facebook's storytelling preference. "Completely changed how I think" promises a transformation story that Facebook audiences engage with deeply.

### Example 19c (TikTok)

**Hook:**
> Stop scrolling. If you run a business, this one mistake is costing you thousands every month.

**Formula:** Pattern Interrupt
**Audience:** Founder-CEO
**Platform:** TikTok
**Why it works:** "Stop scrolling" is the ultimate meta-pattern interrupt -- it commands the exact behavior TikTok users are doing. Direct address ("If you run a business") filters the audience instantly. The dollar-amount consequence ("thousands every month") makes it feel personally urgent.

---

## Mixed Formula Examples (Advanced)

### Example 20 (Stat-Led + Story)

**Hook:**
> I've sent 2,400 cold emails this year.
>
> 11 turned into clients. Here's the template that landed 9 of the 11.

**Formula:** Stat-Led + Story
**Audience:** Founder-CEO
**Framework pairing:** How-To, Personal Lesson
**Why it works:** Combines the credibility of volume (2,400) with the vulnerability of a low conversion rate (11/2,400). The offer of the specific template that drove 82% of wins is an irresistible value promise.

### Example 21 (Question + Contrarian)

**Hook:**
> Why are we still building annual marketing plans?
>
> The market moves in 6-week cycles. Your plan expired before Q1 ended.

**Formula:** Question + Contrarian
**Audience:** Marketer
**Framework pairing:** Contrarian Take, Industry Insight
**Why it works:** The question challenges an entrenched practice, and the follow-up delivers a specific alternative timeframe (6-week cycles) that gives the contrarian take a constructive direction. Marketers in the middle of annual planning will feel this acutely.

### Example 22 (Bold Claim + Pattern Interrupt)

**Hook:**
> The org chart is dead.
>
> The best-run companies I advise replaced theirs with a decision rights matrix. Most teams don't need new structures. They need clarity on who decides what.

**Formula:** Bold Claim + Pattern Interrupt
**Audience:** Corporate-CXO
**Framework pairing:** Industry Insight, How-To
**Why it works:** "The org chart is dead" is a bold declaration that CXOs cannot ignore because org design is core to their role. Introducing "decision rights matrix" as the replacement gives the hook substance beyond provocation.

### Cross-Platform Examples

### Example 22a (X/Twitter)

**Hook:**
> Raised $2M. Spent $1.8M on the wrong things. Here's the $200K that actually mattered.

**Formula:** Stat-Led + Story
**Audience:** Founder-CEO
**Platform:** X/Twitter
**Why it works:** Three escalating sentences that tell a complete micro-story in under 85 characters. The vulnerability of admitting waste ($1.8M) combined with the promise of the distilled lesson ($200K) is a perfect thread opener on X.

### Example 22b (Meta -- Facebook)

**Hook:**
> Everyone told me to hire fast and fire faster. I did the opposite for two years and it's the reason my company survived while theirs didn't.

**Formula:** Contrarian + Story
**Audience:** Founder-CEO
**Platform:** Meta (Facebook)
**Why it works:** Personal narrative challenges widely shared startup advice. The emotional weight of "survived while theirs didn't" is strong on Facebook where emotional resonance drives sharing. The implicit comparison creates both curiosity and identification.

### Example 22c (TikTok)

**Hook:**
> You're about to hear the worst business advice ever given -- and it's something you probably believe right now.

**Formula:** Bold Claim + Pattern Interrupt
**Audience:** Founder-CEO
**Platform:** TikTok
**Why it works:** "You're about to hear" creates anticipation in the first four words. The superlative ("worst ever") raises stakes. "Something you probably believe right now" makes it personal and impossible to scroll past without finding out what it is.

---

## Quick-Reference: Formula-Audience Matrix

| Formula | Founder-CEO | Technical | Marketer | Corporate-CXO |
|---------|-------------|-----------|----------|---------------|
| Stat-Led | Revenue, time savings, team metrics | Performance benchmarks, latency, uptime | Conversion, engagement, ROAS | Market share, growth rates, retention |
| Question | "What would you build if..." | "What if your infrastructure..." | "What's the real cost of..." | "What is your board asking about..." |
| Story | Near-miss business moments | Over-engineering confessions | Campaign wins and failures | Strategic pivot narratives |
| Contrarian | Challenge hiring/scaling norms | Challenge popular tools/practices | Challenge marketing gospel | Challenge org/strategy conventions |
| Bold Claim | "The best founders I know..." | "The best engineering teams..." | "Top-performing campaigns..." | "The companies outperforming peers..." |
| Pattern Interrupt | "Delete/Fire/Cancel your..." | "Your [tool] is lying to you" | "Unsubscribe from..." | "The [sacred concept] is dead" |

## Quick-Reference: Platform-Formula Fit

| Formula | LinkedIn | X/Twitter | Meta (FB) | Meta (IG) | TikTok |
|---------|----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|--------|
| Stat-Led | Strong | Strong | Moderate | Moderate | Strong |
| Question | Strong | Strong | Moderate | Strong | Strong |
| Story | Strong | Moderate | Strong | Moderate | Strong |
| Contrarian | Strong | Strong | Strong | Moderate | Strong |
| Bold Claim | Strong | Strong | Moderate | Strong | Strong |
| Pattern Interrupt | Strong | Strong | Strong | Moderate | Dominant |
